data.title: FX Analysis: USD/JPY Maintains A Clear Range Ahead Of Japanese CPI
data.description:
By Elior Manier

USD/JPY (USD:JPY) is often playing tricks on FX traders, and this time it is completely avoiding volatility after gigantic up-and-down moves.

The currency pair is known for its erratic price action, highly affected by movements in rates, global trade, and inflation, as well as regional and geopolitical developments - all of which have been severely affected since the beginning of the US-Iran conflict.

Seen as a major safe haven currency since the early 2000s, profiting from lower yields in times of panic, JPY could not find any appeal during this conflict.

Even as stock markets initially sold off, risk-off assets and currencies failed to gain traction, with the US dollar and WTI crude drawing all the attention.

Believing the conflict would stay focused on the Middle East, a wider flight to safety was avoided.

But the economic damage to Europe, and in the case of today's USD/JPY outlook, Japan and Asia, is still heavy, and that led to massive rallies in the US dollar against currencies from these regions.

You can see the strong correlation between USD/JPY and oil movements in our recent analysis of the pair.

Add to this narrative a striking stall in inflation in Japan, which was the only path to justify a return to less accommodative policy, and traders really found a natural terrain to race back to Japan shorts.

The Japanese CPI, releasing tonight at 19:30 (ET), is expected to rebound, as supply-side inflationary pressures could once again slowly push Japanese consumer prices higher.

The Bank of Japan mentioned conflict-led inflation a few times but reportedly still leans toward a pause at the upcoming meeting, while hinting at a higher chance of a 25 bps hike in June to allow for further analysis of the war's impact.

So, unless CPI beats expectations by a lot, this pricing shouldn't change much.

With the second round of talks, delayed for almost a week and a half, set to resume tomorrow and continue throughout the weekend, this will be a decisive moment for the FX pair.

Forming a clear 2,000-pip hesitation range in recent action, traders are waiting to see if a proper peace solution is met (implying a break lower in the range) or if the war is to resume, which would add further chances to revisit 2026 highs (above 160.00).

Let's dive right into an intraday time frame analysis for the Gopher - more commonly named USD/JPY.

USD/JPY Multi-Time Frame Analysis

4H Chart

Instead of entering a corrective phase, as was forecasted by the break below key MAs and bull channel, USD/JPY maintained a clearly range-bound picture as the US dollar completely stalled its correction.

Since reaching new 2026 highs on March 27, the pair has been stuck in a clear 2,000-pip range between 157.50 and 159.50 (+/- 100 pips).

While the consolidation is solid, as seen with the flattening 50 and 200 Moving Averages, traders will have to remain cautious, as the narrative could change during the weekend.

Currently at the resistance, USD/JPY has more chances to reject lower, but any headlines regarding a compromised peace process would push for a breakout towards 160.50.

Let's take a closer look.

1H Chart and Technical Levels

As can be seen on the 1H time frame, the range has seen swift up-and-down movement, tumbling to support last Friday and exploding back to retest resistance.

With today's North American session not expected to provide any meaningful change, traders should remain patient.

If the CPI data comes in hotter, expect to see a drop below 159.43 (50-hour MA) which could provide decent sell-stop entries. Extending below 158.80 should see bearish acceleration.

  • Watch out if the action breaks 159.80
  • The weekend break will provide high volatility movement on Monday, so watch your size ahead of the key developments.

Resistance Levels

  • 159.50-159.70 2026 major resistance (range highs)
  • 159.78 daily highs
  • April 2024 160.00-160.40 major resistance
  • June mini resistance 160.70-161.00

Support Levels

  • 159.43 (50-Hour MA)
  • Mid-range pivot 158.75 bull above, bear below
  • December highs major pivot 157.50-158.00 (range lows)
  • 156.00 pivotal support
  • 155.00 mini support

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Otis Worldwide: Lackluster Margin Progress Is A Notable Headwind
data.description:
It’s been a while since I’ve updated my thoughts on leading elevator manufacturer Otis Worldwide (OTIS). Since late 2022, the company has struggled to make sustained progress in two of its key drivers—service attach rates and overall margins—and the result has been a rather lackluster share price performance.

Since that last article, Otis shares are up less than 10%, trailing the broader industrial sector by a wide margin, not to mention direct rivals like KONE (KNYJY) and Schindler (SHNDY) and peers with broadly similar end-market exposures and drivers (service attach, renovation/modernization, et al.) like Allegion (ALLE) and Johnson Controls (JCI).

At this point I’m not really any more bullish on Otis. The potential to do better is certainly there, but potential only gets you so far. Installations should continue to recover in 2026/2027, and I’m still bullish on the opportunity in services, but I need to see better sustained execution to get substantially more bullish on what has historically been a more defensive late-cycle name.

A First Quarter Miss-And-Cut, But Not A Major Miss

It’s still early in the reporting cycle, but I think it’s more likely than not that Otis’s first-quarter results will have them on the wrong side of average for industrials this quarter. As a company that has historically been a late-cycle defensive industrial, that’s not a huge surprise.

Organic revenue growth was only 1%, order growth has slowed, and margins contracted at both the segment and corporate levels, and management nudged earnings guidance a bit lower for the remainder of the year, but with embedded expected improvements in 2H’26 that could be tough to achieve.

Revenue rose 1% this quarter, with new equipment sales down 5% in organic terms and service revenue up 5%. Within new equipment, the EMEA region was the only one to show growth (up low single-digits), with the Americas down low single-digits, China down more than 20%, and Asia down mid-single-digits. For service, maintenance and repair saw 4% growth on 10% growth in repair, while modernization rose 6%. Relative to expectations, new equipment sales were 3% better than expected and service revenue was 1% better, for an overall beat of about 2%.

Gross margin improved 40bp to 30.3% despite ongoing labor, material, and freight cost pressures, and this was over a point better than expected. Even so, operating income declined 2% (6% in constant currency), missing by 2%, with margin down 130bp to 15.4%. Segment profit fell 2% with a 130bp margin decline to 16.7%. New equipment profits fell 42% (margin down 240bp to 3.3%), while service profits rose less than 4%, with margin down 160bp to 23% and missing by over a point.

A Still-Mixed Macro, And With Longer-Standing Margin Questions

Orders rose 4% in constant currency (5% ex-China), while backlog improved 9% (15% ex-China), with order growth slowing from recent quarters. China and Asia remain weak, but new equipment orders in the Americas did improve more than 20%, and modernization orders rose 11% (6% ex-China).

Non-residential construction has yet to really rebound in the U.S., though leading indicators like the Dodge Momentum Index do suggest improvements later this year and into next year. Unfortunately, the construction outlook in China is still not strong, and likewise, there are ongoing concerns about under-absorption of multifamily capacity in many major U.S. markets (China and U.S. multifamily are both areas where Otis is more exposed than peers). Adding to this, the conflicts in the Middle East have had a material impact on the business this year (around 1% to 2% of sales), largely through diminished modernization activity.

One ongoing unknown to monitor is the balance between new equipment sales and modernization activity. While around half of the worldwide installed base is more than 20 years old, and China has been actively encouraging and subsidizing modernization, the path forward is still cloudy. Modernization is more profitable for Otis, but the replace-vs-refurbish decision depends on other factors like interest rates and material/equipment costs—higher rates generally favor modernization, as does a higher-price environment.

A bigger long-term question remains how well Otis can execute on opportunities in the service business. While Otis enjoys leading share in new and installed equipment (in the high-teens to low-20%s recently), service share is in the low double digits. This is not an Otis-specific issue, as independent service providers have a large presence in the elevator space, but it is one that management has been trying to change for years.

Digital services remain a potential share-driving opportunity. Otis offers a variety of tools that allow for remote monitoring and diagnosis, system management, and security (including credentials-based access to floors), and not many independents have the resources to offer competitive offerings. On the other hand, independents are typically willing to accept lower margins than publicly-traded companies want, and with labor being a major driver of service costs, it has been challenging for Otis to compete for more price-sensitive customers.

Why does this matter? New equipment margins are typically in the mid-single digits, while service margins are well into the 20%s. Since my last update, the company has not done quite as well as I’d hoped in terms of improved service attach rates, and that has led to disappointing margin and free cash flow generation relative to my expectations.

One near-term driver to monitor is the possibility of consolidation in the space. There are rumors that Kone and TK Elevator are in advanced talks regarding an acquisition. The combined company would enjoy roughly one-third of the global share, and that could be an issue for regulators, and that will certainly alter the industry.

Both have lower relative service share than Otis, and I would expect that the deal could create some customer and worker attrition, but longer term a stronger rival with more global resources is unlikely to be a net positive for Otis.

The Outlook

My core top-line assumptions haven’t changed all that much. I’m looking for a near-term rebound in non-residential construction to help push revenue growth over the next three years to around 5%, but over the long term I still expect growth in the 3% to 4% range. Improved service mix is, of course, an important factor, and I’ve pulled back my expectations a bit, though I do still expect improved attach rates over time given improved technology-based service offerings from Otis.

On the margin side there has been less progress than I’d hoped, and I’ve reined in my expectations for future improvement accordingly. I do think Otis can get from around 18% EBITDA to 19% over the next three years, but progress much beyond 20% is more of a “show me” story that relies heavily on improving service attach/attrition rates and operational efficiencies. With that, I think it will be challenging for Otis to generate FCF margins significantly above 11%-12% on a sustained basis, though that still supports over 6% long-term annualized FCF growth.

Between discounted cash flow and margin/return-driven EV/EBITDA, I don’t see Otis as particularly undervalued. I can support a forward EBITDA multiple in the high-14s to low/mid-15s, but that only gets me to the low-$90s for a fair value, and discounted cash flow gets me to a similar destination even with a favorable discount rate to reflect the positives of the business model (high proportion of high-margin recurring revenue, strong share, high barriers to entry, et al.).

The Bottom Line

While this reads as a negative article on Otis, I don’t dislike the business itself. Rather, I just think the issue has been, and continues to be, one of mismatched expectations from the Street for what this business can and will do. Otis is a quality name in the space and certainly has opportunities to do better in service, but progress here in recent years hasn’t lived up to expectations, and I believe that puts management’s longer-term margin improvement targets at risk.

At a wider discount to fair value or with more evidence of sustained progress in the service business, I’d probably be more positive. As it stands, I see some potential leverage from improving end-markets over the next year or two and value-based refinements to the service business, but for now it’s not a name I’m considering for my portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron's Rally Is Missing The Bigger Shift
data.description:
Introduction

It’s easy to look at Micron’s (MU) recent rally and assume this is just another memory cycle playing out the way it always does. On the contrary, I believe it is right here when things become really different. There is nothing about just price momentum or the influence of the hype around AI being passed to the semiconductor industry.

Memory used to be just a commodity layer that did not matter much, but now, for the first time in a long time, memory has started acting as a bottleneck for the whole process. It is not something MU just works within but the very center of a whole network of problems that make up the entire structure.

Why “This Time Is Different” Actually Makes Sense

I generally hate that line since it is often the precursor of overenthusiasm. Nevertheless, I feel that the uniqueness of the current situation makes it defensible. Historically, cycles were driven by oversupply leading producers to expand, customers to double-order, inventories to grow, ASPs to drop, and margins to deteriorate.

On top of that, the demand profile used to be associated with slower and more predictable device categories. PCs, smartphones, and classical servers definitely counted, but they did not introduce such urgency for customers that they needed to enter long-term strategic agreements with suppliers. Cost and timing optimization were the main priorities. The demand was high, and the memory segment was indeed crucial, but it lacked true differentiation and scarcity to drive more strategic behavior on the customers' end.

Now this is where the current situation differs from all of this. Hyperscalers require a memory-intensive solution with very tight performance and bandwidth metrics. HBM memory is becoming a critical bottleneck, and this, in turn, drives DRAM and NAND prices higher due to growing needs of inference-related operations. The product mix is different, and so is the type of demand.

This Time the Current Profitability May Persist

Surprisingly, the spectacular Q2 earnings did not lead to any rerating of the stock, and in fact, Micron shares declined somewhat following the call. Two implications follow from that. First, a lot of optimism was already baked into the stock, and second, the market does not quite believe that Micron will keep this kind of profitability going.

That is why the next Q3 quarter is pivotal for the company and may serve as the decisive factor in assessing its prospects in the long term. After all, it has to exceed both its forecast of $19.15 (+/-$0.40) and analyst expectations, which seem to have drifted up to the low $20s. The bar is not set low anymore, and the market expects a beat.

Nevertheless, I still believe that this is achievable. As management itself states, HBM volume is sold out, pricing remains strong, tight supply of DRAM and NAND continues, and the product mix is favorable. In other words, it is entirely possible that the earnings power will prove to be ahead of the modeling expectations once again.

Of course, I would avoid relying on particular metrics too much, but this general picture seems to tell a story. Namely, Micron does not need perfection in order to validate the bull scenario. All it needs is enough evidence that the margin trend stays intact despite the previous heroic results.

It All Comes Down to Monetization of Scarcity

And here is where my thesis lies. What I like the most is that this is not just about revenue or even margins. It is about Micron managing to monetize the current scarcity in a much more sustainable manner than it ever did before. It seems to me that this is what the whole thesis hinges on.

Previously, high prices were unsustainable because there were multiple substitutes, inventories normalized, and product mix offered little support in terms of higher-margin solutions. Now, Micron is dealing with higher value pools, increased strategic engagement of the customer, and a more proactive approach toward forecasting. Here the strategic customer agreement concept is crucial as well.

I do not need the details of those contracts, and yet I know for sure that switching from a normal long-term agreement to a multi-year one with high commitments signals the realization that the memory has become way too important to leave everything up to short-term dynamics and pricing.

It is also important to mention the strategic importance of Micron in a geopolitical environment in which countries care about their semiconductor production and artificial intelligence infrastructure. I am not saying that it should be overstated in this situation, but this consideration definitely adds some value.

The Valuation Case Has Room for Much Sharper Rerating

Here comes the most uncomfortable part for the bearish case. Despite the recent bull run, MU trades at absurdly cheap multiples relative to earnings. Specifically, at $487, the company costs approximately 8 times FY2026 consensus EPS at ~$57 as well as under 5x FY2027 consensus near $101. These multiples appear to be unrealistically cheap if the current peak will be only temporary.

However, I believe that the valuation needs to be based on earnings power. Namely, one needs to consider the multiple that corresponds to this kind of earnings growth, margin expansion, and strategic importance with the current tightness in supply. Even at a conservative 7 times FY2027 EPS, the stock price goes above $700. At 8.5 times, it approaches $605. That is the reason why the range between $500 and $600 appears to be reasonable if the Q3 quarter confirms the ongoing trend.

What Can Go Wrong

In any case, the most obvious risk that comes to mind is the possibility of this turning out to be an elongated super-cycle rather than a structural re-rating. Indeed, if the demand of hyperscalers declines, memory requirements per system plateau, or if inference efficiency improves, the current scarcity premium may dissipate much quicker than the bulls expect. This is quite problematic considering how highly dependent the current profitability of the company is on it.

Moreover, the HBM share remains competitive in this situation, and any issues regarding qualification or the change in the customer base will hit Micron even harder than anticipated. While rivals SK Hynix and Samsung are not standing idle, the company also has plans to increase its CAPEX in FY2026 to above $25 billion, with additional steps up expected. Should this result in overbuilding at the tail-end of the cycle, the price compression may be brutal.

Another thing is the risk related to sentiment. As demonstrated recently, beating forecasts does not automatically lead to positive price movements. Thus, even despite being bullish, I cannot say that the upcoming ride will be smooth. This is not typically true for memories.

Conclusion

I still believe that Micron remains among the best AI infrastructure mispricings in semiconductor large caps. It’s not Micron’s earnings per se that make me bullish but rather their quality. For the first time in a while, there is a scarcity theme behind the profits that appears to be strategic, not cyclical. All it takes is for this theme to persist, even only partially, and the current multiple does not capture the earnings potential.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: The Case To $40 Is Here (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Kinder Morgan (KMI) is a company I closely monitor because I thought it would become a direct beneficiary of the new American-European LNG diplomacy. My rating was neutral because I said that the stock was already reflecting the positive outlook, but I wrote that it would have exited its trading range if a new catalyst appeared. That was in January. The rest is history: the Iran War started, and the stock exited its range, moving up to $35 before retracing back a bit.

Now that Kinder Morgan has released its Q1 2026 earnings, it's time to once again assess the company and see whether the stock deserves a rating upgrade or not.

Kinder Morgan: A Quick Overview

Most of us are familiar with the company. But allow me to share just two visuals that help us understand at a glance what the company is. First of all, it is an infrastructure company. In the U.S. it holds the number one spot among natural gas transmission networks, since it moves more or less 40% of the U.S.'s natural gas production. It is also working on storage capacity, having over 700Bcf (roughly 15% of the U.S. capacity). This is going to make it an important company, especially as we are getting used to volatility in gas and oil demand. Natural gas is its largest segment, accounting for two-thirds of the mix, while refined products make up another 26%.

In addition to its hard asset advantage, Kinder Morgan also benefits from predictable cash flows. As we can see below, around 65% of its cash flow architecture comes from take-or-pay contracts, which require customers to pay regardless of the actual throughput. Another 26% is fee-based, which means that it is generated regardless of commodity price. Only 4% of the company's cash flow is exposed to commodity price volatility. This is why I have always liked pipelines over wells: they are more stable.

With this in mind, let's look at the earnings report.

Kinder Morgan's Q1 2026 Earnings Review

Kinder Morgan topped consensus estimates. The main driver can be easily guessed: disruption in the Middle East is shifting demand toward the U.S., which is exporting a rising volume of natural gas and LNG.

The company reported $4.8B in revenue and non-GAAP EPS of $0.48. The adj. EBITDA was $2.54B vs. my forecast of $2.3B. The only miss seems to be the FY26 EPS guidance, which is just $0.03 shy of the current $1.39 that investors expect. I think it is only a matter of time: we will see Kinder Morgan beat this guidance if things continue as they are now.

When we look at the segment operations, we see that natural gas pipelines increased their transport volume by 8%, with a 90% utilization rate. LNG demand is a structural tailwind that we'd better not overlook when we assess KMI. Products pipelines saw refined product volume down 2%, but this was because a pipeline was converted from oil to gas. Without this, volumes would have been up 2% YoY.

There are three main catalysts that are changing the picture. First of all, facilities in Qatar are under attack and surrounded by uncertainty. As a result, U.S.-sourced LNG is becoming more valuable and more requested by the day. Secondly, data centers are driving up power demand. Interestingly, we learned during the earnings call that the generation capacity forecasted to be required just a year ago has now doubled:

... utilities plan to add a staggering number of 153 gigawatts of gas-fired generation capacity in the next several years, primarily to serve data centers, with the bulk of this coming online by 2030. Now this is twice the estimate by the same group of 1 year ago and reflects plans to build about 210 additional natural gas-fired facilities.

When people say that data centers are a speculative tailwind, it would be better to point to the numbers that quantify what we are talking about.

Finally, Kinder Morgan is in expansion mode, and it now has a $10B project backlog (two years ago, the company was at $3B). In addition to new construction, Kinder Morgan is also acquiring new assets, usually utilities and industrials with investment-grade ratings. An example is the acquisition of Monument Pipeline for $500M.

Overall, the expansion is surely driven by rising demand, but it is also carried out thoughtfully with a clear goal in mind: financial sustainability and a timeline where most projects should be EBITDA accretive by mid-2028.

Moody's certified the disciplined approach by upgrading KMI to Baa1. This is an important milestone, since just a decade ago, Kinder Morgan was close to junk territory. Now, its debt/adj. EBITDA ratio is 3.6x, which is extremely conservative for a big infrastructure player. It also improves Kinder Morgan's access to bond markets while lowering its interest rates.

Valuation

Kinder Morgan is not cheap, as its D+ valuation grade on Seeking Alpha shows. The expected EBITDA for this year should be around $9.4B, which leads to an EV/EBITDA ratio of 11.1 (based on an EV of $104.35B). This is a multiple that, although not cheap, is not expensive either. It fits with my base case scenario. A bear case would be triggered by an EBITDA figure close to $9.10. Here, the multiple would compress to 10x, leading to a share price of $26. On the other hand, a bull multiple could be 12x based on an EBITDA of $9.8B. This would push the price up to $38.

However, I think the stock deserves to be upgraded to a buy. I think the premises are there to see its EBITDA grow and reach $10B by the end of this year and $10.5B at the end of FY2027. With a base case multiple of 11x, we would already have a new target price of $35. If we expand the multiple to 12x, we have a target price close to $40. This represents 27% upside, not counting the dividends that KMI pays on a quarterly basis (fwd yield 3.7%).

For sure, Kinder Morgan doesn't trade range-bound anymore. Its tripling backlog and the structural tailwinds that I described above shift the stock from a bond proxy to an infrastructure growth play. Therefore, the risk/reward ratio is now favorable, and I think that the stock has become a buy.

data.title: CNX Resources: Due For A Re-Rating With Earnings Set To Beat
data.description:
CNX Resources Corporation (CNX) has been a decent performer in 2026, up about 6%, outpacing the broader market. In the past few months, however, the stock has begun to quietly trail off. The company will report earnings on April 30 next week, and it's time to take a better look at the potential for upcoming results, especially following a very solid Q1 in terms of natural gas prices that were buoyed by cold weather and multiple large storm systems in the northeast.

Business & Financial Profile

CNX is a natural gas exploration and production company primarily operating in the Appalachian Basin, which consists of Pennsylvania, Ohio, West Virginia, and Virginia. With the rise in demand for electricity over the past few years, carrying with it higher prices, the fuel mix required has shifted towards natural gas, which now comprises nearly 40% of all PJM electricity mix.

The company has two key segments - gas production and liquids production. The vast majority of revenue and production volume comes from gas, which is nearly 91% of the mix. Gas is exactly what it sounds like - the production of natural gas. Liquids are commonly referred to as NGLs and include things like ethane, propane, and butane.

What caught my eye for CSX was the free cash flow profile. The company has had 24 consecutive quarters of free cash flow growth, and 2026 looks to be no different. The company is forecasting approximately $550 million in free cash flow for the year ahead. At the current market price, that's nearly a 10% free cash flow yield and the best free cash flow result the company has produced in the post-COVID era.

Source: Investor Presentation

This prompts further investigation. Recall their guide for 2026:

  • Production volume: 605-620 Bcf.
  • Prices on open volumes (not hedged): $3.51/MMBtu for gas (post-56c differential for the Appalachia) and $20.75/Bbl for NGL.
  • Adjusted EBITDAX: $1.31-$1.36 billion.
  • Free cash flow: $550 million.

The hedging view is important to figuring out our estimates for the upcoming quarter. For the upcoming quarter, the average price per Mcf is hedged at $3.68/Mcf with a production volume hedged amount of 86.5 Bcf. In addition, the company also has the physical hedges (non-exchange hedges) of 25.4 Bcf at $2.67/Mcf. This combines to produce approximately 110.8 Bcf of gas at an average price of $2.86/Mcf. This is the actual realized hedge price for these volumes.

In a given quarter, the company can produce nearly 140 Bcf of gas, so the hedged amount is quite high.

Source: BamSEC

Thus, in trying to determine the spot price remainder and being cognizant that this is an Appalachia-based E&P company, we can take a look at that specific differential relative to the core pricing standard, Henry Hub. Given the winter storms that occurred this past quarter, there was a rather large spike in the price for gas.

Source: Aegis Hedging

At one point, because of the storm surge in the Appalachia during January/February, the differential versus the Henry Hub price was almost nothing. This should help to support prices in the quarter but should otherwise be pretty similar to last quarter in terms of the realized differential versus Henry Hub.

During the quarter, the average Henry Hub price was $4.71/MMBtu versus last quarter's $3.72/MMBtu. This is a sequential increase of 26% and a y/y increase of 13.5%. Last Q1's average price was $4.14/MMBtu.

Source: Trading Economics

Forward Estimates

In attempting to triangulate Q1, we start with the revenue for all production, both gas and NGLs. The company has a small production exposure to oil, but it is essentially negligible for our purposes.

Given the spot prices for natural gas, their hedging guidance, and the guidance on derivative contracts in their last 8-K, we arrive at $456 million in natural gas revenue and $54 million in NGL and oil revenue. Their loss on the derivative contract is likely going to be somewhere around $88 million after accounting for a VWAP NYMEX Henry Hub price. This is likely subject to the most variability in their revenue build. Purchased gas revenue (20 Bcf x $4.50/MMBtu) should be $90 million, and other revenue follows simply a historical run rate and is much more stable, amounting to about $35 million. This produces an estimate of about $547 million for the quarter in total revenue. This is relative to Street looking for $511.6 million, so I expect a clean beat.

For operating expenditures, things can swing wildly depending on the derivative result in the revenue build, so using a baseline opex ratio isn't appropriate. Much of the individual expense items in the opex build follow consistent ratios against Natural Gas, NGL + Oil combined revenue (i.e., pre-hedging, other, and purchased gas revenues). I have made certain assumptions about items like transportation, gathering, and compression costs, as well as production fees and core SG&A, in order to arrive at a total opex figure of $361 million.

After working through the below-the-line items, we arrive at $1.05 in EPS versus the street at $0.91. There is a risk for further upside depending on how transportation costs come in. I chose to model higher transportation costs this quarter, accounting for quarters where there are surges in price that tend to carry higher ratios. I am modeling 22% of core natural gas sales as the transportation cost, which is elevated versus last quarter's 19.8% ratio. If it follows more like last quarter, then the EPS beat will improve to $1.12 versus the street at $0.91. The quarter should be very strong given the commodity price performance, and I expect a clean beat.

Valuation

With the company set up for a solid Q1 performance, it's time we turn to the valuation to determine if there is upside here.

In looking at relative valuation, there are a few we can use:

  • EQT Corp. (EQT) trades at 6.0x EV/EBITDA with a forward P/E of 12.2x.
  • Range Resources (RRC) trades at 6.2x EV/EBITDA with a forward P/E of 10.4x.
  • Antero (AR) trades at 9.2x EV/EBITDA with a forward P/E of 9x.

On consensus estimates, CNX trades at 6.0x EV/EBITDA and 13x earnings. Now, it's important to consider with the valuation of CNX that P/E is not the right multiple to be using, same for its peers. There are often many below-the-line items, particularly asset sale-related or other costs, and differences in leverage profiles across the space mean EV/EBITDA is the best barometer for valuation.

I think it's appropriate that the company starts to get a 7x EV/EBITDA multiple, let alone 8x EV/EBITDA with a 10% free cash flow yield forecast and the commodity price tailwinds to support it. I see little reason why, if Q1 earnings are robust, the stock shouldn't re-rate here.

So, while the stock has had a great run, it's off the highs and trailing below its 50-day moving average. A strong earnings report could help send shares back towards the highs. I am looking for a $51 price target and the stock to trade at 8x EV/EBITDA. The stock is also heavily shorted, with nearly 14% short interest. This is less a function of investors being negative on CNX but more that because CNX hedges out the majority of its commodity price risk (>80%), it doesn't participate in the upside as much as other pure-play natural gas E&P, which creates bullish spread trades in the space should one have that view.

Source: Passage Research

Risks

As a natural gas E&P, the company is subject to swings in the price of natural gas. This means that the company has real weather risk, and seasonal shifts or unseasonable weather can cause material impacts to the spot price of natural gas. Next, the company, like many E&Ps, maintains a robust hedging program. There can be no future guarantee that hedged volumes are robust enough to ensure steady financial performance nor that the hedges result in value accretion for shareholders, as it is subject to management risk in how they lock the prices ahead. Third, the company is essentially a pure-play natural gas E&P. Should natural gas prices trend at a low level for a long amount of time, it could materially affect the company's operations.

Conclusion

CNX looks well-positioned to beat Q1 earnings given commodity price tailwinds in the quarter and a strong hedging book. The outlook for the year is bolstered by very strong free cash flow guidance, which implies that the stock has a near 10% free cash flow yield at the current level. This is one of the best levels of free cash flow in the company's history, making the current 6x EV/EBITDA valuation considerably cheap. Just off the highs, the stock is a buy.

data.title: Confidence In 'Rules Of The Game'
data.description:
The problem with the U.S. economy today, according to Amit Seru, a finance professor at the Stanford Graduate School of Business, is “restoring confidence in the rules of the game.”

Mr. Seru goes on, “The deeper challenge facing American capitalism today…is restoring confidence in the rules of the game.”

The implication?

President Trump has destroyed “the rules of the game.”

Trump has imposed the “Art of the Deal” on the economy…and this has busted up “the rules.”

Consequently, “There will be work to do once Trump leaves office…”

But, “perhaps the hardest task of all will be imposing the discipline required to ensure that policymakers do not do too much all at once…” in trying to re-establish the rules.

The strategy behind the “Art of the Deal” is breaking the rules…is creating situations that force people to make “deals”…is creating situations that force people who are not necessarily ready to move…to move.

The rules of the game create trust. They contribute to stable and foreseeable actions.

The rules of the game result in a community.

Just look at what Jerome Powell has done over the past four years leading the Federal Reserve.

Now, Mr. Powell can be criticized for a lot of things, especially building up the securities portfolio of the Federal Reserve during the early stages of the COVID-19 pandemic.

Take a look at this!

Do note, however, that the movements of the Federal Reserve are not radical and/or unstable movements. Once begun, the Fed establishes a pattern that the commercial banks can rely on, and so the whole program can move forward with the commercial banks building up “trust” in the behavior of the Fed.

And this can be said after the Fed turned and began its effort of quantitative tightening.

I believe that he did a very commendable job in attempting to reduce the size of the Fed's securities portfolio over the past four years.

Starting in the middle of March 2022, the Federal Reserve began its program of quantitative easing.

The Federal Reserve announced the program.

The Federal Reserve began the program.

And the Federal Reserve carried out the program…with no surprises…with no turnarounds…just steady, trustworthy behavior.

The U.S. economy, once the “shock” was over, grew at a steady pace, inflation stabilized, and the rate of unemployment stopped rising.

The economy actually performed quite well during this period of time.

There was plenty of money in the commercial banking system to support the economic expansion.

The economy grew, driven by the supply side.

And, in November 2025, Chairman Powell announced that, starting in December, the Federal Reserve would convert from a program of quantitative tightening to one of quantitative easing.

And so it did!!!

The Federal Reserve has been able to move through the last four years, trying to reduce the excess amount of reserves it had put into the banking system during the spread of COVID-19.

I believe that Mr. Powell and the Federal Reserve could not have done the job they did during this period of time without having announced their program and then carried it out without any unannounced changes.

Reducing the size of the securities portfolio was the primary focus of the Federal Reserve during this time.

The second part of the program pertained to the Fed's policy rate of interest…the federal funds rate.

The Federal Reserve program included changes in the Federal Funds rate, but these changes were to come after the movement took place in the securities portfolio.

The Federal Reserve only moved the federal funds rate “after” market conditions changed and the Fed officials could justify a change in the policy rate.

As it worked out, this is how the Federal Reserve moved its policy rate of interest during this time of quantitative tightening.

Notice that during the first part of this policy program, the Federal Funds rate was rising.

The Federal Reserve was “tightening,” and at this stage in the program, the Fed's policy rate of interest needed to be raised.

In the second part of the program, we see that the Fed's policy rate of interest needed to be lowered.

And so Mr. Powell and other Federal Reserve officials lowered the policy rate.

Market participants “trusted” what Mr. Powell and the Fed were doing, and so the markets adjusted smoothly.

And this continued, as on the first of December, Mr. Powell and the Fed moved from a policy stance of quantitative tightening to a policy stance of quantitative easing.

But something else is now in the air.

President Trump wants lower interest rates.

Trump wants to get the economy growing faster.

Trump wants to “Make America Great Again.”

But his approach is to achieve the goal using the “Art of the Deal.”

And this is where Mr. Seru comes in.

Mr. Powell and the Federal Reserve established the “rules of the game.”

Trump wants to get what he wants by demolishing the “rules of the game.”

That is exactly where we are.

Mr. Powell will be leaving the Fed.

Trump has nominated Kevin Warsh to become the Chairman.

This means change.

Currently, we are trying to figure out what this change might be.

Mr. Seru believes that there will be substantial changes to the rules.

Consequently, behavior like that created by Jerome Powell and the Federal Reserve will be gone.

Greater uncertainty with more volatility will result.

And, according to the analysis of Mr. Seru, the return to “rules” will take time, and the effort to “return to the rules” is an unknown path and will contain its uncertainty and volatility.

Furthermore, the “trust” that has been built up will disappear and require a whole new pattern of confidence-building.

And it will require a whole new team of confidence-builders.

data.title: GE Vernova: Integrated Power And Electrification To Delivery High-Margin Growth
data.description:
GE Vernova Inc. (GEV) has mapped out for margin-accretive growth across Power and Electrification, underpinned by a 5-year investment outlay of $1.4T by the utilities sector to expand, modernize, and harden the grid. With data center growth far from stalling, I believe GE Vernova’s opportunity may be undervalued by the market, particularly when considering its integrated power generation, transmission, distribution, and orchestration infrastructure, positioned to effectively facilitate a microgrid for dedicated capacity. With the expectation of positive pricing power and higher production over the coming years, I am remaining bullish on GEV shares, reiterating my Strong Buy rating with a price target of $2,138/share at 43.83x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

GE Vernova Operational Update

GE Vernova started FY26 on a high note as it nears its $200b backlog target, adding $13b in Q1 ’26 for a total backlog of $163b. Backlog growth in the quarter was largely driven by equipment orders, adding $76b to the backlog across Electrification, Power, and, to a lesser degree, Wind.

Strong demand for gas turbines is driving positive pricing pressure for GE Vernova, which may translate to stronger margins in the coming years as management remains prudent in managing a lean organization. Accordingly, management is expecting 10-20% in price increases for power equipment for new orders in 1H26 when compared to the close of FY25, acting as a positive growth catalyst for the top line and margins. In Q1 ’26, GE Vernova signed 21GW of gas turbine capacity, bringing its capacity under contract up from 83GW in Q4 ’25 to 100GW. New orders for the Power segment totaled $10b for a book-to-bill ratio of 2.01x. Operationally, the power segment delivered durable top-line growth with a 469 bps adjusted EBITDA margin expansion when compared to the previous year’s quarter. In addition to this, capacity expansion is expected to begin driving revenue in Q3 ’26, delivering 20GW of annualized output. Further out, GE Vernova will be adding an additional 4GW of manufacturing capacity in 2028 to facilitate the high level of orders through the end of the decade and into the 2030s.

Driven by increasing power demand, the Electrification segment experienced strong tailwinds in Q1 ’26 with $7.1b in new orders for a book-to-bill ratio of 2.40x. Underpinned by increased utility capital deployment to support grid modernization, stability, and expansion, GE Vernova reported segment revenue growth of 61% and a 621 bps expansion to the segment’s adjusted EBITDA in Q1 ’26. The forecast currently stands at $1.4T in investments by utilities over the next 5 years to support domestic power demand, up from an estimated $1.1T reported in 2025 through 2030.

In addition to utility investments, data centers are delivering growth for the Electrification segment given that these gigawatt sites will house high-voltage substations, switchgear, storage, conversion, and distribution equipment given the high level of power capacity at these sites. In addition to equipment sales, GE Vernova is gaining momentum for its orchestration and automation software to facilitate stable power distribution.

Taking a step back, the big picture strategy for GE Vernova is the ability to offer a fully integrated power solution, from generation to transmission, distribution, and orchestration, providing an impactful offering for utilities and data center customers. Accordingly, 20% of GE Vernova’s turbine backlog in Q1 ’26 was attributed to data center customers, meaning that these sites will likely be dedicated to the data center site. If this is the case, this will effectively require the data center to operate and orchestrate a microgrid to ensure a stable power supply that matches power requirements at the data center site.

Data center capacity is expected to double from 2025 to 2030 to 200GW of power demand. In the U.S., data center capacity is expected to grow at a 17% CAGR through 2030, effectively requiring a substantial uplift in power generation to support the growing footprint. Asia Pacific is expected to grow at a 19% CAGR in a similar fashion, despite near-term headwinds in terms of gas supply resulting from the war with Iran. Management outlined that APAC orders shouldn’t be impacted by short APAC LNG supply, given that the facilities the gas turbines will be placed in will not likely be commissioned until the early-to-mid-2030s, providing a long runway for alternative LNG export capacity to come online. I’m personally expecting the U.S. and Latin America to facilitate growth in LNG export capacity as safer alternatives to the Middle East, eliminating the bottleneck that is the Strait of Hormuz.

Another growth catalyst to consider regarding the data center theme is the transition to 800-VDC architecture for the next-generation AI factory in the coming year. With the new distribution architecture to support higher power capacity server racks, higher voltage electrical equipment may be required to facilitate these high-demand sites.

Wind is expected to be a mixed bag for the next two years, resulting from the expected conclusion of the Dogger Bank offshore project in mid-to-late 2027. Offsetting high costs resulting from the subsegment, onshore wind experienced improved margins, though the market remains subdued. The Wind segment closed Q1 ’26 with $1.2b in new orders for a book-to-bill ratio of 0.84x.

GE Vernova Financial Position & Forecast

Looking ahead to Q2 ’26, I’m forecasting GE Vernova to generate $10.7b in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $3.48/share. I’m forecasting growth to primarily derive from Electrification and Power, offset by a decline in Wind. In addition to this, I’m forecasting margins to continue to improve in eFY26 as a result of stronger pricing power, particularly as electrical equipment remains in short supply in the U.S.

GE Vernova closed Q1 ’26 with $10b in cash & equivalent and $2.8b in debt on the balance sheet for a net cash position of $7.4b. Management outlined that capital deployment for 2026 will largely entail facility expansions to service capacity demand in Electrification and Power. Given the high growth in Electrification, I suspect the investment cycle may continue over the coming years as demand for equipment remains heightened.

Risks Related To GE Vernova

Bull Case

Demand for electricity remains strong, driving significant margin-accretive order intake across the Power and Electrification market segments. Through its integrated grid offerings, I believe GE Vernova has the potential to realize an uplift in demand from data center operators seeking to own a dedicated power supply, potentially delivering on GE Vernova’s integrated management solutions that can power and orchestrate a microgrid.

Bear Case

If the investment cycle for data center developments were to stall, GE Vernova could be faced with a major setback in Power and Electrification, particularly as it invests more capital into expanding manufacturing capacity.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

GEV shares are currently trading at a rich premium of 82.35 EV/aEBITDA following a major share price run-up post-Q1 ’26 earnings. Despite the high trading premium, I believe the value is justified given the long runway for high-demand electrical and power generation offerings, presenting an appealing case for long-term durability for services in the coming years.

Fundamentally, I believe GEV shares should be priced at $2,138/share at 43.83x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA; I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: Traeger: The Performance Is Cooked, But There Is Hope
data.description:
Introduction

Traeger’s (COOK) performance since its IPO back in 2021 has been nothing but a disaster. Its share price is down over 96% in just 4-5 years, but it looks like there may be something happening in terms of a turnaround. However, it is too early to tell and definitely not worth gambling on for now.

Briefly On Performance

Starting from the top, over the last three years, the company saw a boost at one point, but then that dissipated by the end of 2025.

In the latest quarter, Q4 ’25, revenues came in at around $145.4 million, down another 14% y/y, but it did beat estimates by $10.35 million. Grill revenues decreased 22.3% to $60.6 million, which makes sense since it was the winter season. Consumables revenues increased almost 16% in the latest quarter to $35.5 million, while accessories revenues decreased 18% to $49.2 million. I expect this to pick up in later quarters, but I don’t think they are going to break the overall downtrend.

Looking at the company’s profitability, margins across the board have improved dramatically since mid-’23. Back in 2023, the company was dealing with post-pandemic inventory correction, with retailer destocking and weak consumer demand, but management was very proactive during that time and right-sized inventories. These efforts helped simplify the business, which led to the improvement you see below. The company was deep in the red and continues to be so, but it is close to pulling itself out of the hole. The company’s restructuring and goodwill charges put it deep in the red at the end of FY25.

There’s no point in looking at other efficiency metrics like ROA and ROE because the bottom line is in the red, so they would be in the red as well. Let’s move on to the company’s financial position. Traeger finished the year with around $19.6 million in cash and equivalents against $400 million in debt. That is a lot of debt for what has become a rather small company with $101 million in market cap. This in itself is quite risky, especially when the operations are not doing as well as they ideally should be. Interest expense on that debt alone takes $31.3 million a year, and with the way current operations are going, Traeger cannot cover these interest payments because it is making a substantial loss. The cash on hand is also not enough to cover the annual interest expenses. On a positive note, we can see that its EBITDA margin is positive right now, which could satisfy the creditors for now, as it does look as if it is on its way to a turnaround. Adjusted EBITDA was around $70 million, which is comfortably higher than its interest on debt. No imminent default, but if things get worse, the risk rises.

Additionally, the company’s cash flow from operations was around $20.5 million, and free cash flow came in at $13.5 million. At least they are still positive; however, these have continued to decline and are much lower than they were back in 2023.

Overall, I do see some positives in the overall operations of Traeger. The management has done a decent job of turning EBITDA-positive over the last few years, but that is essentially the only positive so far, because its revenue continues to decline, it is still not turning GAAP profit, and it has a lot of debt on its books that can become rather risky.

Comments On The Outlook

Project Gravity

This is the company’s strategy of streamlining operations and delivering cost savings across its operations. It was introduced back in 2025 and is being rolled out in two phases through the end of 2026. Phase 1 focused on workforce reductions and integrating MEATER into its core operations. This phase is meant to drive $30 million in annualized cost savings once it is fully implemented, while Phase 2 was meant to bring another $20 million annually through a broader simplification process across production channels, supply chain, and manufacturing.

One way that the company is trying to revitalize its performance is through channel optimization. The second phase is going to see the company exiting its Costco (COST) roadshow. It appears that the company viewed the roadshow as not enough of an ROI to justify continuing it. The economics at scale were not attractive enough, and that is the reason for Project Gravity: to cut the fat and streamline operations. Another part of Phase 2 is exiting DTC, which was less profitable because of the overall supply chain costs, especially when it's last-mile delivery. Instead, the company will now focus on its retail partners and redirect all the traffic to their websites. I admire the efforts of simplifying everything, even if it means giving up some control and some revenue. These are the steps to take if it wants to survive and reduce the debt load.

I am rooting for the company’s turnaround, and I hope that the actions that the management is yet to take will drive a meaningful reduction in wasted capital and efficiencies. The company desperately needs to find its footing once again if it is to survive going forward. I think it just needs some time and patience.

The risks remain real. The company’s share price plummeted considerably, which forced the management to do a 1-for-50 reverse stock split to comply with the minimum share requirements. It was trading under a dollar for a while, and even with the split, the company’s share price continued to decline. Its share price is down close to 97% since its IPO back in 2021. That is a quick fall.

Should You "Buy" Traeger?

If you are hoping for a quick turnaround play here, I don’t think you’ll get it. The company continues to underperform under every metric; the demand for its grills is just not there, and that might mean the end of premium grilling. I am a fan of the product, but I think the reason it is not working out is the fact that consumers are a lot more financially cautious. I couldn’t justify dropping over $4,000 on a smoker or even $2,000, unless I’m retired and smoking meats is my sole hobby in life.

The management still has a long way to go, but I am encouraged by the progress. I could see the company turning a profit over the next couple of years if it has time. Analysts are already expecting the company to turn profitable by the end of 2027. At that point, it would be trading at around a 10x forward PE ratio.

I’m going to put the company on my watchlist so I can follow the whole situation, but I don’t see buying into it right now.

data.title: Toast: A High-Growth Specialist With AI Tailwinds
data.description:
Thesis

Toast (NYSE: TOST) is one of the more compelling risk-reward situations I see in the mid-cap growth universe right now. The stock is caught in a trap of three things that put off investors: payments, consumer spending, and enterprise software, and this has brought the shares down a notch from their 52-week highs. Despite this, the firm has been executing far better than it has in years. Locations are up 22% over 2025 to around 164,000, annual recurring revenue is over $2.0 billion, and the company is layering on AI-enabled tools that may strengthen its position in each restaurant that it services. With a multiple that already gives the market, it is pricing in a significant consumer slowdown. I value Toast as a resilient, high-quality compounder selling at a meaningful discount to its optimal foreseeable return.

Company Overview

Toast began with a straightforward pain point: three founders tired of waiting forever for their restaurant check. That frustration became a laser-focused thesis: a cloud-based platform for the hospitality industry that integrates POS hardware, payment processing, and software modules for payroll, inventory, marketing, and online ordering.

Originally targeted at small and mid-sized restaurants within the United States, the company has expanded into various countries around the globe, including the United Kingdom, Ireland, Canada, Australia, and larger enterprise-style customers. As of the end of 2025, Toast powered a total of approximately 164,000 restaurant locations around the globe, a net growth of just over 30,000 locations during the course of the year—representing a record number of locations gained for the company.

Operating Momentum

The main thesis can be divided into three parts: scale, mix, and embedded AI.

Scale and Growth

Toast published revenues of $6.15 billion in 2025, an increase of 24% over the previous year. That is no triple-digit growth, but still attractive topline growth for a business of this magnitude (mid-teens to low-20s). As a more significant indicator, the ARR band - Toast's most significant metric - was reported at $2.05 billion in Q4 2025, an increase of 26% over the previous year, with a four-quarter average growth rate around 29%. This is the sort of “compound recurring revenue” that investors have been willing to pay a premium for, regardless of the current valuation.

Mix & Margins

Payment volume continues to be the driver, but Gross profit is growing at a faster rate than revenue. Gross payment volume for 2025 was $195.1 billion, +23% Y-Y, which feeds in directly into Toast's top line. Meanwhile, software & Fintech are gradually increasing as a percentage of the stack, which should keep gross margins steadily expanding over time. Toast is not simply “selling terminals”; it is shifting to a higher-value, more recurring product pyramid.

AI and Workflow Stickiness

Toast has started integrating AI into workflows operators already use, instead of creating an analytics layer for operators to adopt alongside their existing tools. For example, Toast IQ, Toast's conversational AI assistant, enables operators and restaurant owners to use an AI-enabled medium to make network-wide menu recommendations, operational meta-units and seat-count visibilities, and marketing integrations to boost the bottom line. One of the first network-wide implementations of Toast IQ was done at a high-volume venue like Carmine's in Times Square, where Alicart was able to use Toast IQ insights to make menu combination adjustments, which yielded increased check sizes. That concrete ROI makes AI justifiable in a real-world situation.

Why Toast Is a Compounder

Toast derives its power from the ability to compound economics in a fragmented, non-tech-savvy vertical.

Each restaurant also brings recurring software revenue, payment volume, and data. The more data Toast gains from 160,000+ locations, the more it can compare performance, advise menu adjustments, and improve deals, and the more it can charge for incremental components. This is as traditional a ‘data moat’ as you get, but given that no independent pizzeria owner is going to develop their own AI platform from the ground up, Toast's ingrained position is far less vulnerable.

This is reinforced by the enterprise angle. Toast has signed deals with Uber and with large chain restaurants such as Applebee’s, MTY Food Group (Papa Murphy’s), Teriyaki Madness, and Alicart Restaurant Group (Carmine’s, Virgil’s BBQ). These are not secondary outlets; these are flagship restaurants taking thousands of covers a day. This validates the size of the platform and its performance in high-pressure conditions.

Competitive Landscape

Toast is not alone. Key competitors include:

  1. Square/Block (XYZ): Wide given payments and POS landscape with eatery highlights and its very own AI push.
  2. Clover/Fiserv (FISV): Has strength in SMB hardware and software and access to Fiserv’s distribution network.
  3. Lightspeed Commerce (LSPD): Focuses on hospitality and international markets, acquiring companies that extend its footprint in the restaurant stack.

They are all investing in restaurant-focused, AI-enabled tools; any of them could choose to slash margins to win share. That is a genuine threat. But that laser focus on restaurants gives Toast a deeper level of domain expertise and a more tightly integrated product than horizontal payment platforms that also serve food businesses. That is its moat.

Catalysts and Recent News

On the bullish side:

Enterprise momentum: The Alicart partnership, the Teriyaki Madness rollout, and the deployment of Papa Murphy's all happened in relatively quick succession, pointing to the enterprise sales motion speeding up for Toast.AI implementation: Toast IQ is being deployed within the existing base and, if shown to boost throughput, lower order error rates, or raise average check sizes, would constitute a switching cost-increasing retention lever. Brand perception: Toast made it on Fast Company’s World’s Most Innovative Companies list for 2026, which may sound mushy but in the long run matters when you are looking at enterprise procurement cycles.

On the cautious side:

  • Insiders selling: There has been considerable selling in recent months. This may have harmless reasons (vesting, tax planning), but it is worth mentioning.
  • Macro sensitivity: Toast's payment processing is still the lion's share of gross profit even if restaurant spending takes a meaningful hit.
  • How quickly you can grow through acquiring customers: As a result of heavy entry into enterprise and international markets, the customer acquisition cost payback period became negative in the last quarter. Hence the growth, through value creation for new customers, is at its greatest rate. (The company has to keep an eye on this, however, as measures are already being taken to support the growth).

Valuation

Post the drawdown, Toast now trades at a much lower multiple than when the stock was trading at its peak. Toast, on consensus estimates, is trading at somewhere around the 2.3x forward revenue mark, which is a fairly conservative growth multiple for a company that is delivering over a 29%+ ARR growth rate across a large customer set. Wall Street is currently estimating Toast to grow 20.5% in 12 months, a slowdown from the roughly 26% 2-year compounding but still reasonable growth for such a large company.

If Toast can sustain mid-teens to low twenties revenue growth over the next five years (capitalized by location expansion, higher software attach, and international rollout), keep shifting its mix towards higher-margin software and fintech services, and use AI to optimize retention & cross-sell, then the current multiple begins to appear more like a discount to its long-term story than a vote of no confidence. The market is assuming a consumer recession at a forward revenue multiple of only 2.3x for a platform growing at a 29% ARR growth rate.

Risks to My View

I would seriously reconsider my bullish stance if spending at restaurants goes into a structural slowdown serious enough to pull GPV growth back into single digits. This will both diminish revenues into the margin and deter new openings where unit economics would be positive.

I see risks too if the cash flow payback period for new customer acquisition remains in negative territory for 2+ quarters in a row, indicating Toast is losing its effectiveness to attract new customers.

Another risk arises if one of Toast's largest competitors (Square or Clover) releases several key AI-enabled features that effectively commoditize Toast's differentiation in the software space and erode its moat.

Conclusion

Toast gets the macro bits right: payment, consumption, and software. That’s why I think it’s inexpensive. Yet the business keeps opening new sites at record levels, wins enterprise customers to bolster the platform advantage at scale, and builds an AI-enabled data moat that most restaurant-facing rivals are years away from doing. Trading at only 2.3x forward sales, the stock already discounts a massive slowdown in growth. If the U.S. consumer holds up and Toast pulls through, patient investors have a multi-year compounder under an apparently struggling name.

data.title: IXN: All You Need To Know About This Global Tech ETF
data.description:
Introduction to the iShares Global Tech ETF

The iShares Global Tech ETF (IXN) is a $7.5B-sized financial product (by assets under management) that is issued by Blackrock, Inc. under their ‘iShares’ brand of ETFs. IXN, which initially got listed in November 2001, is priced at an expense ratio of 0.39%, and offers an annualized distribution yield of less than 1% (distributions are made twice a year).

What Does IXN Do?

IXN is a passively managed ETF that attempts to track the performance of an index crafted by another independent party (S&P Global) called the S&P Global 1200 Information Technology 4.5/22.5/45 Capped Index [SG1IT]. SG1IT, in turn, is a subset of the S&P Global 1200 index [SG1], and seeks to pick out stocks from the latter which are defined as “information technology”. Note that SG1 captures the top 70% of the overall global market cap (making it tilt mainly to giant and large-caps, while it also weights its constituents on the basis of their free float market-cap) and picks up stocks from specific regions such as the US (represented by the S&P 500), Europe (S&P Europe 350), Japan (S&P TOPIX 150), Canada (S&P TSX 60), Australia (S&P/ASX All Australian 50), Asia (S&P Asia 50), and Latin America (S&P Latin America 40). Based on the indices mentioned above, it looks like SG1IT attempts to source tech stocks from all global regions except Africa, and the Middle East.

SG1IT, which rebalances its constituents four times per year, also seeks to impose certain caps on individual holdings to ameliorate concentration effects. For instance, during each quarterly rebalance, no single stock is permitted to account for more than 22.5% of the entire index, and all stocks with individual weights of over 4.5% are not permitted to “jointly aggregate” to 45% of the total index.

It appears that IXN, which covers 127 stocks in total, does an acceptable job of tracking its target index, as its tracking errors across different time periods are in line with the median ETF levels.

What Are the Dominant Traits of IXN's Portfolio?

IXN may have the word “global” in its nomenclature, and while it certainly does dabble with stocks from at least six different foreign countries across the world, at the end of the day, this is still very much a US-centric product, with 75% of the portfolio domiciled domestically.

Then, IXN covers businesses from across the technology spectrum, but broadly they can be bracketed into three buckets:

  • Semiconductors and manufacturers of semiconductor equipment
  • Technology hardware businesses
  • Software & services

Of these categories, nearly half the portfolio comes from the first bucket.

Since IXN’s tracking index is a float-adjusted market-cap weighted index (put simply, stocks with a higher market-cap receive a higher weight in the index), there’s little surprise to note that this is a product that is overwhelmingly dominated by giant and large-caps which account for over 90% of the portfolio. Meanwhile, the average market-cap of over half a trillion dollars only reiterates the strong giant-cap positioning of IXN.

Then, even though IXN covers 127 global tech stocks and seeks to impose certain caps on its individual holdings, that doesn’t take away from the fact that this is a top-heavy portfolio with just the top 5 (or the top 4% of the holdings by number)- Nvidia (NVDA), Apple Inc. (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) jointly accounting for a mammoth 43% of the entire portfolio by value.

Stylistically, over half of this portfolio consists of growth-style stocks (stocks characterized by strong sales and earnings growth, price momentum over months, those that reinvest excess cash into the business rather than reward shareholders by way of dividends or share repurchases, etc.), although there’s also a useful component (over one-third of the portfolio) of blended stocks which don’t just have the typical growth characteristics, but also some value traits such as relatively cheap valuation multiples, strong dividend payouts, etc.

What Are The Risks Associated With IXN?

Investors who like to see steady consistent returns from their portfolio holdings, may not take comfort from the erratic nature of IXN’s return profile. To elaborate, while your typical ETF tends to see annualized volatility of 13% in a year, IXN’s volatility profile is considerably higher at over 21%.

For further insight, take a look at the image below which shows how divergent IXN’s tracking index's annual returns have been over the last decade. Even over a span of just two years (2022/2023), we’ve seen wild swings from a -30% drawdown in the first year, to a +54% profit in the following year!

Also take note of IXN’s relatively high sensitivity to the movements of the S&P 500. Over the past three years, IXN’s beta over the prime equity benchmark has worked out to 1.22x, so this tells us that for every 1% movement in the latter, the former can be expected to move by 1.22%. This could prove to be lucrative during a bull run, but the reverse is true during a bear market, making IXN something of a concern, when risk aversion in the markets are on the wane.

Given the caps that IXN seeks to put in place every quarter, and given the strong interest for US tech mega caps (which could end up expanding their market-caps and relative exposure within IXN’s portfolio), this is an ETF that is going to be susceptible to plenty of transaction activity, which won’t be particularly efficient, if you own it in a taxable brokerage account. To provide further context, while the median annual turnover ratio for an ETF is less than 30%, IXN’s annual turnover ratio is 1.5x more at 45%, and the constant rebalancing is likely to ramp the transaction costs which could also end up eating into the returns.

Technology stocks are typically not known for being generous with their distributions to shareholders, and investors of IXN should not be under the impression of assuming that this ETF (which focuses on these tech stocks) is embarking on a new path of distribution largesse. To shed more clarity, note that in December last year, IXN’s total distributions (recall, it makes these distributions twice a year) spiked by a massive 360% YoY at $0.93 per share. However, if investors breakdown that distribution, they will note that it was not because IXN’s holdings decided to distribute higher dividends (in fact, relative to December 2024, the income component actually contracted by -13%), but rather because they saw a substantial capital gain of $0.75 per share. Since, IXN tracks an index that imposes caps on its holdings, it will be forced to sell its holdings once those caps are breached resulting in a long-term capital gain. All, in all, if one just looks at the annualized dividend income (excluding capital gains) facet of IXN, which is more representative of its dividend profile, it only equates to a yield of less than 0.3% (well below the median ETF yield of 2.74%).

Investors who are mindful of themes of mean reversion in the financial markets, may be troubled to note that global “tech” stocks, look like one of the most overextended counters within global equity markets (the current relative strength ratio of IXN to a more diversified basket of global stocks is more than double its long-term mean of 0.39x and may be vulnerable to some downward mean reversion pressure going forward.

Who Is IXN For?

Since IXN is still largely dominated by US-domiciled technology stocks, it’s fair to say that this product would be suitable for those who appreciate a strong tilt towards the US technology bucket (which is still largely seen as the hub of the global innovation), without getting entirely wedged to that pocket (25% of IXN’s portfolio consists of global technology stocks which offers some element of diversification). These investors also recognize that the technology supply chain, particularly with the rapid penetration of artificial intelligence is no longer primarily centered in the US, and is also gradually picking up steam in other regions across the world, and want to also gradually profit from that movement.

Investors who want a pool of high growth stocks that are not necessarily the priciest from a valuation angle (more commonly known as GARP-style stocks, or those offering growth-at-a-reasonable-price) will likely also fancy a portfolio like IXN. Compared to its sister product- the iShares US Technology ETF (IYW), which covers 139 domestic technology stocks or the most popular growth ETF- the Vanguard Growth ETF (VUG), IXN’s holdings are poised to deliver the most favorable long-term earnings growth (19%). Higher growth usually translates to higher valuation multiples, but in this case, IXN is the cheapest out of the lot, priced at an earnings multiple of 18x which implies a 15% discount over US tech, and a 30% discount over US growth.

IXN will also appeal to investors who like cost-efficient ETFs; despite having to fish for stocks from across the world, which is not particularly straightforward and easy to procure, IXN is priced at a reasonable expense ratio of only 0.39%, which is around 11bps cheaper than the median ETF expense rate of 0.5%.

Peers of IXN

Besides IXN, two other ETF alternatives focusing on the global tech landscape are the iShares Future Exponential Technologies ETF (XT), and the The SP Funds S&P Global Technology ETF (SPTE).

XT is also issued by Blackrock and looks like a more futuristic product as it covers both developed and EM businesses involved in the development of exponential technologies that will likely displace older technologies (the coverage of XT is wider than IXN, but its average market-cap suggests that it is a mega-cap focused fund). Like IXN, XT while global in scope, still mainly tilts to the US (69% exposure), and is also prone to the same degree of churn. XT also offers a stellar trailing yield, but like IXN the most recent distribution isn’t driven by income, but by capital gains (in XT’s case, it isn’t just long-term capital gains, but short-term capital gains as well.

SPTE, which is relatively new (set-up only in 2023), and issued by Shariah funds, tracks a global tech index (only half the portfolio is US-based, offering solid diversification) that is Shariah-compliant (composed in accordance with Islamic law). SPTE, which doesn’t witness as much of churn as the other two, also offers the useful feature of monthly distributions, however when those distributions are annualized, it isn’t too compelling. Note however that SPTE is relatively more expensive to own, suffers from weak daily liquidity, offers the lowest coverage, and is heavily concentrated in its top 10 holdings.

Summary

IXN, covers 127 technology stocks, with a lion’s share of the portfolio based in the US. This product comes across as reasonably priced (both from an expense ratio angle, as well as the valuations of its holdings), offering solid earnings growth potential. Investors should be mindful of IXN’s relative high volatility, turnover, and heightened sensitivity to the key US equity benchmark.

This article answers three main questions about IXN:

  1. What are the key features of IXN's portfolio?
  2. What type of investor is IXN suitable for, and what are the risks associated with it?
  3. What are the global tech ETF alternatives to IXN?
